text.title: DDD ACL Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules grounding the Anti-Corruption Layer pattern.

rule.statement: The Domain layer MUST define the interface (`InventoryChecker`, `PaymentGateway`) using domain types and Ubiquitous Language only. The interface MUST NOT import the external client SDK or any infrastructure type.
rule.rationale: Domain-owned interface prevents the external model from leaking; tests can stub the domain interface without spinning up SDK dependencies.

rule.statement: The ACL implementation in the Infrastructure layer MUST be the ONLY file that imports the external SDK / client / vendor types. Domain and Application layers MUST NOT reference vendor SDKs.
rule.rationale: Multiple SDK importers fan out vendor coupling; swapping vendors requires touching every importer. One ACL = one swap point.

rule.statement: The ACL MUST translate external error types into domain exceptions (`InventoryUnavailableError`, `PaymentDeclined`). Raw vendor exceptions (`StripeError`, `requests.HTTPError`) MUST NOT propagate past the ACL boundary.
rule.rationale: Domain code reacts to domain errors; leaking vendor exceptions couples error handlers to vendor versioning and obscures the actual domain meaning.

rule.statement: For non-critical integrations the ACL MUST define a fail-safe default (e.g. "if inventory API unreachable, assume available with a retry-after-N-minutes log entry"). The chosen default MUST be documented in code and in the runbook.
rule.rationale: Without a documented fail-safe, every outage in the dependency becomes an outage in the caller; explicit policy makes the trade-off visible.

rule.statement: The ACL MUST have at least one contract test pinning the external system's response shape (mocked HTTP server / cassette). Domain code MUST have unit tests against the domain interface (mocked).
rule.rationale: SDK upgrades silently change response shapes; pinned contract tests fail fast when the upstream contract drifts.
